tt_insn_vec.help: Portion of vector registers that is actually used. Available only for instructions belonging to a SIMD/vector instructions set (like x86 AVX)

tt_Recip_throughput.help: Average number of cycles to retire that instruction if repeated (assuming independent instructions, with no read-after-write dependency)

hint.details:
These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
 - VMOVUPD: 1 occurrences<<list_path_1_complex_1>>
hint.title: Complex instructions
Type of elements and instruction set
hint.txt: 7 AVX-512 instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (eight at a time).

hint.title: Matching between your loop (in the source code) and the binary loop
hint.txt:
The binary loop is composed of 80 FP arithmetical operations:
 - 56: addition or subtraction (24 inside FMA instructions)
 - 24: multiply (all inside FMA instructions)
The binary loop is loading 472 bytes (59 double precision FP elements).
The binary loop is storing 64 bytes (8 double precision FP elements).

hint.title: Arithmetic intensity
hint.txt: Arithmetic intensity is 0.15 FP operations per loaded or stored byte.

cycles | 0.50      | 0.50 | 0.25 | 0.25 | 0.50 | 6.67 | 6.67 | 6.67 | 5.00 | 3.00 | 4.00 | 5.00 | 1.00 | 1.00

Execution ports to units layout:
 - ALU0/BRU0: ALU, BRU
 - ALU1: ALU
 - ALU2: ALU
 - ALU3: ALU
 - BRU1: BRU
 - AGU0 (256 bits): store address, load
 - AGU1 (256 bits): store address, load
 - AGU2 (256 bits): store address, load
 - FP0 (256 bits): VPU, DIV/SQRT
 - FP1 (256 bits): VPU, DIV/SQRT
 - FP2 (256 bits): VPU
 - FP3 (256 bits): VPU
 - FP4 (256 bits): FP store data
 - FP5 (256 bits): FP store data

expert.help: Compare the CQA-computed load/store/compute metrics with the capacity of the core. For instance if CQA computes 24 bytes in the loop in 2 cycles (that is 12 cycles per cycle) and if the core can process up to 16 bytes per cycle, that metric is 75%.
expert.title: Cycles and memory resources usage
expert.txt:
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each iteration of the binary loop takes 6.67 cycles. At this rate:
 - 73% of peak load performance is reached (70.80 out of 96.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
 - 15% of peak store performance is reached (9.60 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

expert.help: List of bottlenecks detected by CQA. A bottleneck is an issue which reduces front-end throughput below the core capacity.
expert.title: Front-end bottlenecks

header: 50% of peak computational performance is used (12.00 out of 24.00 FLOP per cycle (GFLOPS @ 1GHz))

gain.workaround:
- Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:
  * use march=native
 - Use vector aligned instructions:
  1) align your arrays on 64 bytes boundaries: replace { void *p = malloc (size); } with { void *p; posix_memalign (&p, 64, size); }.
  2) inform your compiler that your arrays are vector aligned: if array 'foo' is 64 bytes-aligned, define a pointer 'p_foo' as __builtin_assume_aligned (foo, 64) and use it instead of 'foo' in the loop.
gain.details:
93% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):
 - 90% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
 - 75% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.
Since your execution units are vector units, only a fully vectorized loop can use their full power.
gain.title: Vectorization
gain.txt:
Your loop is highly vectorized.
94% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By fully vectorizing your loop, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 6.67 to 6.42 cycles (1.04x speedup).

gain.workaround:
- Read less array elements
 - Write less array elements
 - Provide more information to your compiler:
  * hardcode the bounds of the corresponding 'for' loop
gain.title: Execution units bottlenecks
gain.txt:
Performance is limited by:
 - reading data from caches/RAM (load units are a bottleneck)
 - writing data to caches/RAM (the store unit is a bottleneck)

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 6.67 to 5.00 cycles (1.33x speedup).
